design_philosophy.core_loop: Raise/lower terrain to provide buildable land → followers build houses → population grows → mana increases → unlock new divine powers → use powers to expand territory and eliminate enemy followers → progress to next level
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Asymmetric population growth and resource management: player must balance terrain manipulation, follower expansion, and strategic use of limited divine powers against an opposing deity with similar capabilities
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Acting as a deity who shapes the world and guides civilization through direct intervention and landscape manipulation; the power fantasy of god-like control over nature and population
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Pioneering god game that strips traditional RTS complexity in favor of direct terrain deformation as the primary strategic tool; rejects unit production queues in favor of organic follower growth based on available land; emphasizes emergent systems where simple rules (peeps build on flat land) create complex strategic depth

difficulty.continues_system: Level-based progression; failure on a level requires restart of that specific level
difficulty.checkpointing: Level-by-level checkpoint system; over 500 discrete levels provide granular progression checkpoints
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: High agency through divine power selection and timing; player must learn when to deploy which spells (earthquakes, floods, swamps, volcanoes, knight conversion) and manage mana carefully; landscape manipulation decisions are entirely under player control

progression.upgrade_system: Mana-gated power unlocks: as follower population increases and builds more houses, mana meter rises, progressively unlocking additional divine powers (earthquakes, floods, swamps, volcanoes, knight transformation)
progression.unlock_mechanism: Automatic mana threshold system; powers unlock as accumulated mana reaches specific levels during gameplay
replayability_hooks: 500 distinct levels with varied landscape types (desert, rock, lava, snow, ice) that affect follower development differently
Multiple divine power combinations allow different tactical approaches to the same level
Enemy AI provides variable challenge across playthroughs
Multiplayer support (mentioned in Wikipedia) enables head-to-head deity competition

world_building_techniques: Isometric landscape visualization presented as an open book world map
Landscape biome variety (desert, rock, lava, snow, ice) creates implicit world regions and visual storytelling
Follower behavior (building on flat land, avoiding water barriers) creates emergent narrative of civilization development
Divine power theming (earthquakes, floods, volcanoes) positions player as active mythological force

boss_design.philosophy: No traditional boss encounters; instead, each level culminates in a final population-based conflict where the player's accumulated followers must overwhelm the enemy deity's followers in direct confrontation
notable_bosses: The opposing deity (AI-controlled, varies per level) - adaptive challenge that grows with player's civilization
Level-specific environmental hazards (swamps, volcanoes, lava) function as environmental obstacles rather than traditional bosses

faction_indicators: Player faction vs. opposing deity faction; visual representation through follower population counts
Landscape control as territorial dominance indicator
Divine power usage patterns reveal player's strategic philosophy
danger_signals: Enemy mana accumulation and power unlocks indicate escalating threat level
Rapid enemy population growth signals player is losing territorial advantage
Environmental hazards (enemy-created swamps, volcanoes) indicate opponent strategic aggression
environmental_storytelling: Raised terrain indicates player's civilization centers and expansion patterns
Flattened/excavated land shows resource extraction and infrastructure zones
Water barriers and natural features become implicit story elements of land friction
Divine power scars (earthquake damage, flood zones, lava flows) visually narrate the conflict history

sources_used: Wikipedia: Populous (video game) - gameplay mechanics, core loop design, development philosophy, level structure
